spec_match.rationale: Three patterns each call getOrInsert / getOrInsertComputed exactly as the TC39 proposal-upsert spec ships — the API set the feature actually ships.

interactive.rationale: Pill-style pattern switcher updates editable input → live before/after code panels → live output table. Real interactivity, not a static card.

blurb_alignment.rationale: Feature index promises 'three patterns that get one line shorter and one bug-class smaller' — counter, group-by, dedup — and the page delivers exactly those three with editable input.

edge_cases.rationale: Each pattern has a polyfill fallback for missing methods, but no demo of what happens on empty input, single-token input, or extreme inputs (e.g. 10k words for performance flavour). The dedup pattern stores `firstSeen` timestamps but never surfaces them.

references.rationale: See-also section links to https://chromestatus.com/feature/5201653661827072 and tc39/proposal-upsert.

design_system.rationale: Variables used throughout; rose/emerald boxes carry --text-black foreground (AA-clean). Buttons inherit the global --text-black background + --bg-ivory color rule correctly.

openQuestions.title: Use the displayed `firstSeen` data in the dedup output
openQuestions.detail: The dedup pattern records `firstSeen: performance.now()` inside the cached value but the output table only shows the count. Surfacing a `first seen at` column would (a) make the value-side richer than a bare integer and (b) demonstrate why getOrInsertComputed is useful when the default value is composite, not just a primitive.

openQuestions.title: Show the bug-class the new API eliminates
openQuestions.detail: The blurb claims 'one bug-class smaller'. Adding a callout to each pattern showing the off-by-one or undefined-handling bug in the manual version (e.g. `m.get(w) + 1` returning NaN when w is absent) would teach the safety benefit, not just the brevity.

openQuestions.title: Add an empty/single-token edge case
openQuestions.detail: An 'edge inputs' button row (empty string, single token, unicode word) would show that getOrInsert handles these without surprise; right now the demo only really runs against the canonical pangram.

openQuestions.title: Cover the WeakMap counter pattern
openQuestions.detail: All three patterns use Map. A WeakMap variant (e.g. per-DOM-node click counter, using event.currentTarget as the key) would broaden the angle and double as a hook into the WeakMap surfaces of the spec.

summary: Strong before/after demo for the three canonical Map.getOrInsert use cases. The dedup pattern under-uses the rich value it stores, and the page never touches WeakMap — a follow-on concept covering object-keyed counters would round out the spec coverage.
